# Transcrição: 07-Injecting Storytelling Into Your Business

- **Fonte:** 07-Injecting Storytelling Into Your Business
- **Duração:** 07:43
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:06]

Business owners, corporate folk, anyone who is trying to launch some enterprise of any kind, they often ask me, why are we talking about personal stories? I want to tell the story of my business. I want to tell the story about my product. I want people to hear the story of my service. All of those things are great and we're going to talk about them. But it all starts with personal stories. The storyteller with the most stories is the best storyteller. And when you have an enormous number of personal stories to lean into, suddenly everything you say can be framed through story. Let me give you a couple examples. I'm working with a company right now that is in love with its data. It is a biotech firm that produces enormous amounts of data and they use that data to justifiably cure diseases and save the world. When it comes time to tell stories about what they do, they talk about their data. They show PowerPoint presentations with 60 slides. Every single slide dents with text, charts, and graphs. And so they come to me and they ask me to help them tell stories. I'm working with one of those data scientists. He's explaining to me that the competitors in his field sort of deal with one problem, produce one product, and ask the customers to modify that product for their needs. The differentiator for this company is that they actually customize their product for all of the problems. So they're making more customers happy, but it's more work. And so he shows me the data on this and he says, how do I make this a story? And I ask him, have you ever been in the grocery store? And he says, yes. I said, have you ever stood in front of that display of apples? Like there's 14 different varieties of apples in front of you. And he says, yes. I said, is it accurate to say that your competitors deal with Macintosh apples? And if their customers want to make pies, two-band, deal with the Macintosh apples and do what you need to do to make them work for you, where your company addresses all of the apples. And he lights up. He says, yes. That's a great metaphor. And I say, okay, but it's just a metaphor. It's not a story. I say to him, do you have children? He says, yes. I say, do your children have apple preferences? And he says, actually they do. He's got two kids and each one likes a different apple. And I say to him, can you not see a story here? Can you not see a story about a father who wants to make his children happy? And he wants to offer them the varieties that they want to eat instead of forcing a variety that they don't eat down their throat.

## [03:06 - 06:10]

Could you not tell a story about trying to be a great father and keeping his kids happy and then shift that story into the work you do? He says to me, how did you do that? And I say, I didn't do anything. I asked you if you had a story and you said yes. And now we know what you need to tell. Storytellers are people that think about their lives, find moments in their lives of meaning, and apply it to the work that they're doing. That's all we do. And so when you have stories, when you're looking at your life, when you're examining and collecting this content, suddenly when you have to present data in some complicated way, you can now do it through a story. I'm working with another company filled with a bunch of scientists who are also obsessed with their data. Rightfully so. They're collecting important data for important reasons. One of the scientists is explaining to me that when they built this machine for the first time, nobody really wanted it. So he says to me, I had to pitch it to a bunch of companies and finally I got a big pharma company to agree to give it a test. And then he moves on with his talk. When he's done, I say, can you talk to me about pitching to the big pharma company? And he says, oh, actually I put the machine in the back of a van and I drove up and down the east coast, banging on the doors of big pharma companies. Sometimes they wouldn't open the door, but I actually had people come out into the parking lot and watch me demonstrate the machine. Sometimes they allowed me to bring the machine into the building and demonstrate it. Sometimes people climbed into the van to watch the machine in operation. He says, that was the way I convinced people that my machine was right. And I asked him, why didn't you tell me that story when you said I pitched my machine to pharma companies? And he said, that sounded like the boring part. I told him, that was the only not boring part. I explained to him, you're a man who drove up and down the east coast in a minivan with a pharma machine that's going to change the world. Legitimately going to change the world and no one would listen to you. And so you dragged CEOs and scientists and researchers into parking lots and into your own van to run the machine for them. I said, you don't think that that demonstrates your level of commitment, your bootstrapping nature, your belief in what you were doing. And he said, yeah, it did, but I just thought that was the boring part of the story. I help people, I'm helping you see that you already have the stories to tell. You find personal stories in your everyday life.

## [06:10 - 07:43]

Once you find them, we find ways to integrate them into the story that you're trying to tell about your company, about your product, about your service. When we combine those two things together, what you do with a story, a personal story, an authentic, vulnerable, meaningful story. When we bring those two things together, suddenly hearts and minds will open for you. People will listen to you. You'll become entertaining. You will become compelling. You will become convincing. You will become connective to the people that you're speaking to. We don't connect to people through data. We don't connect to people through charts and graphs. We don't connect to people through corporate language, corporate speak. All of those things may be necessary in a presentation, but the way we connect to people, convince people, the way we become compelling and most importantly, the way we entertain people while we're delivering our information is through story. I'm going to show you how to take the stories of your lives and make them work for you in the business that you do. Combining the two together, it's a superpower. It's going to change everything that you do. You're going to become a storyteller in the business world. Those people are almost impossible to find. You are about to become one of them.
